prompt: Which player holds the NHL record for most career points, with 2,857?
explanation: Gretzky's 2,857 points is nearly 1,000 more than the second-place finisher on the all-time list.

prompt: Which player is second on the NHL's all-time career points list, with 1,921?
explanation: Jaromir Jagr's 1,921 career points ranks second only to Gretzky.

prompt: Which player holds the NHL record for most career assists, with 1,963?
explanation: Gretzky's assist total alone would be enough to lead the all-time points list even without any goals.

prompt: Which record did Wayne Gretzky achieve on New Year's Eve 1981, scoring 5 goals in one game en route to his 92-goal season?
options: Fastest player to 50 goals in a season (39 games)
explanation: Gretzky scored 5 goals against Philadelphia to reach 50 goals in just 39 games, a record that still stands.

prompt: As of the mid-2020s, has any player matched or beaten Wayne Gretzky's record of reaching 50 goals in just 39 games?
options: No, the record still stands unmatched
explanation: Gretzky's 39-game mark to 50 goals remains unmatched, though several modern players have come close.

prompt: Which modern-era goaltender set a record shutout sequence of 332 minutes and 1 second during the 2003-04 season?
explanation: Brian Boucher's 332:01 scoreless streak with Phoenix set a modern-era NHL record.

prompt: Which goaltender holds the all-time NHL record for consecutive shutout minutes with 461:29, set in 1928?
explanation: Alex Connell's 461-minute shutout streak in 1928 remains the all-time record from the dead-puck low-scoring era.

prompt: Which goaltender recorded a rookie-record 22 shutouts in the 1928-29 season for Montreal?
explanation: George Hainsworth's 22 shutouts in a single season is an NHL record that will likely never be broken given modern scoring rates.

prompt: Which defenseman scored the second-most career goals ever by a player at his position, behind only Ray Bourque?
explanation: Paul Coffey's prolific offensive career ranks him among the highest goal-scoring defensemen in NHL history, behind Ray Bourque.

prompt: Which defenseman holds the NHL single-season goal-scoring record for the position, with 48 goals in 1985-86?
explanation: Paul Coffey's 48-goal season for Edmonton remains the record for defensemen.

prompt: Which defenseman set a single-season points record for the position with 139 points in 1970-71?
explanation: Bobby Orr's 139-point season is the all-time record for a defenseman.

prompt: Which player scored the fastest hat trick in NHL history, netting three goals in just 21 seconds?
explanation: Bill Mosienko's three goals in 21 seconds for Chicago in 1952 remains an unbroken NHL record.

prompt: Which team did Bill Mosienko score his record-setting 21-second hat trick against in 1952?
explanation: Mosienko's historic hat trick came in the final game of the 1951-52 season against the Rangers.

prompt: Which player holds the record for most points in a single game with 10 (6 goals, 4 assists)?
explanation: Darryl Sittler's 10-point game for Toronto against Boston in 1976 remains the NHL record.

prompt: Which player set the NHL playoff record for most points in a single game, with 8 points (5 goals, 3 assists) against Philadelphia in 1989?
explanation: Lemieux's 8-point playoff explosion for Pittsburgh in 1989 remains the Stanley Cup playoff record.

prompt: Which team set the NHL record for most goals scored by a single team in one game, with 16 in 1920?
explanation: The Quebec Bulldogs scored 16 goals against Toronto in a 1920 game, an NHL record for a single team.

prompt: Which player, playing for Quebec in that historic 1920 blowout, scored a since-unmatched 10 goals in a single game?
explanation: Joe Malone's 10-goal game in January 1920 remains an NHL record that will almost certainly never be broken.

prompt: Which goaltender is the only one in NHL history to score a goal by shooting the puck the length of the ice into an empty net, doing so twice in his career?
explanation: Martin Brodeur is the only goaltender to score two goals in NHL history, both by shooting into an empty net.

prompt: Which goaltender was the first in NHL history to score a goal, doing so in the 1987-88 playoffs by shooting into an empty net?
explanation: Ron Hextall became the first NHL goaltender to score a goal, notably doing so in a playoff game.

prompt: Which player is credited with scoring the first-ever legal goal in NHL history, in December 1917?
explanation: Dave Ritchie is generally credited with the NHL's first goal, scored for the Montreal Wanderers.

prompt: Which player recorded the fastest goal from the start of a game, scoring in just 5 seconds?
options: Doug Smail (tied with others at 5 seconds)
explanation: Multiple players, including Doug Smail, Bryan Trottier, and Alexander Mogilny, share the record for fastest goal from the opening faceoff, at 5 seconds.

prompt: Which goaltender holds the NHL career shutouts record overall, with 125?
explanation: Brodeur's 125 career shutouts is the all-time NHL record.

prompt: Which goaltender's career shutout total of 103 stood as the NHL record for over three decades before Martin Brodeur broke it?
explanation: Sawchuk's 103 shutouts stood from his retirement until Brodeur passed him in 2009.

prompt: Which goaltender was the first to regularly wear a mask, doing so starting in 1959?
explanation: Jacques Plante's decision to wear a mask after a facial injury changed goaltending forever.

prompt: Which goaltender was famously the last in the NHL to play regularly without a mask, retiring in 1974?
explanation: Andy Brown was the last maskless goaltender to play in the NHL.

prompt: Which player was the first European-trained skater to win the Hart Trophy as NHL MVP?
options: No European-trained skater has won it; this scenario is false
Sergei Fedorov (1994) was among the first
explanation: Sergei Fedorov's 1994 Hart Trophy win was a landmark moment for European-trained players in the NHL.

prompt: Which was the first European country to have a player selected first overall in the NHL Draft?
explanation: Mats Sundin, drafted first overall by Quebec in 1989, was the first European-trained player picked first overall.

prompt: Which Soviet-trained player was famously drafted by Calgary in the 1983 draft despite being unable to defect until years later?
explanation: Several top Soviet players were drafted by NHL teams years before they were able to actually join the league amid Cold War tensions.

prompt: Which Soviet player became the first to play in the NHL directly from the USSR, joining Calgary in 1989?
explanation: Sergei Priakin's arrival in Calgary marked the first instance of a Soviet player being allowed to join the NHL directly.

prompt: Which Soviet player defected to North America in 1989, becoming one of the first prominent Soviet stars to do so before the fall of the USSR?
explanation: Mogilny's defection to Buffalo in 1989 was a landmark event, occurring before official Soviet permission for player transfers existed.

prompt: In what year was the Calder Memorial Trophy, honoring the NHL's top rookie, first established?
explanation: The Calder Trophy was established in 1937 in honor of former NHL president Frank Calder.

prompt: Has any true NHL rookie ever led the league outright in scoring to win the Art Ross Trophy?
options: No, no rookie has ever won the Art Ross Trophy outright
Yes, Wayne Gretzky did in his rookie year
Yes, Sidney Crosby did in his rookie year
Yes, Teemu Selanne did in his rookie year
explanation: No true NHL rookie has ever led the league outright in scoring, a testament to how difficult first-year scoring titles are.

prompt: Which player set the NHL record for most points by a rookie defenseman, with 76 in 1986-87?
explanation: Gary Suter's rookie season for Calgary set a scoring record among first-year defensemen.

prompt: Which defenseman's dominant rookie season in 1966-67 for Boston, though not a record in every statistical category, immediately announced him as a generational talent?
explanation: Bobby Orr's rookie season began a career that would redefine the defenseman position.

prompt: By what final score did the New York Rangers defeat Vancouver in the decisive Game 7 of the 1994 Stanley Cup Final?
explanation: The Rangers won a tense Game 7 by a 3-2 score in regulation to end their 54-year title drought.

prompt: Which player scored the legendary triple-overtime series-ending goal for Pittsburgh against Washington in the 1996 playoffs, on a wraparound?
explanation: Petr Nedved's goal in the fourth overtime ended one of the longest games in NHL history.

prompt: Which was the longest game in modern NHL history, decided in the sixth overtime period, played in 2000 between Pittsburgh and Philadelphia?
options: Keith Primeau scored the winner for Philadelphia after 5 overtimes (152 minutes)
The game was called a tie
explanation: Keith Primeau's goal after five overtime periods ended one of the longest games in modern NHL history at over 152 minutes.

prompt: Which is the longest game in NHL history overall, played in 1936 between Detroit and the Montreal Maroons, decided in the sixth overtime?
options: Mud Bruneteau scored the winner for Detroit after 176 minutes of play
The game ended in a scoreless tie
It was decided by penalty shots
explanation: Mud Bruneteau's goal after 176 minutes and 30 seconds ended the longest game in NHL history.

statement: Wayne Gretzky holds the NHL record for most career assists, a total that alone would rank him among all-time scoring leaders.
explanation: Gretzky's 1,963 career assists exceed the total career points of every other player in NHL history except himself.

statement: Bill Mosienko holds the record for fastest hat trick in NHL history, scoring three goals in 21 seconds.
explanation: This 1952 record for the Chicago Black Hawks has never been broken.

statement: Darryl Sittler holds the NHL record for most points in a single game, with 10.
explanation: Sittler's 6-goal, 4-assist game against Boston in 1976 remains the record.

statement: Joe Malone scored 10 goals in a single game in 1920, a record that still stands.
explanation: This feat occurred in the NHL's very early years and remains unmatched.

statement: Martin Brodeur is the only goaltender in NHL history to score a goal.
explanation: Brodeur scored twice, but he was not the first — Ron Hextall was the first NHL goaltender to score a goal, in the 1987-88 playoffs.

statement: Jacques Plante was the first NHL goaltender to regularly wear a mask.
explanation: Plante began wearing a mask regularly in 1959 after suffering a facial injury.

statement: Sergei Fedorov was the first European-trained player to win the Hart Trophy as NHL MVP.
explanation: Fedorov's 1994 MVP award was a milestone for European-trained players in the league.

statement: The longest game in NHL history was decided in the sixth overtime period in 1936.
explanation: Mud Bruneteau's goal after over 176 minutes of play ended the longest game ever.

statement: Bobby Orr holds the single-season points record for a defenseman, with 139 points.
explanation: Orr's 1970-71 season remains the standard for defensemen offense.

statement: Mats Sundin was the first European-trained player selected first overall in the NHL Draft.
explanation: Sundin was picked first overall by Quebec in 1989.

clues: Canadian center
Holds the NHL career points, goals, and assists records
Nicknamed 'The Great One'
Scored 50 goals in just 39 games in 1981-82
explanation: Gretzky's offensive records remain untouched, decades after his final NHL game.

clues: Canadian winger
Scored 3 goals in 21 seconds, the fastest hat trick in NHL history
Played for the Chicago Black Hawks
Achieved this feat in 1952
explanation: Mosienko's record-setting hat trick remains one of the most remarkable individual feats in hockey history.

clues: Canadian center
Set the NHL record for most points in a single game (10)
Played for the Toronto Maple Leafs
Recorded this feat against Boston in 1976
explanation: Sittler's 6 goals and 4 assists in one game remain unmatched in NHL history.

clues: Canadian goaltender
First NHL goaltender to score a goal
Achieved the feat in the 1987-88 playoffs
Played for the Philadelphia Flyers
explanation: Hextall's pioneering goal changed how fans thought about goaltender involvement in offense.

prompt: Order these historic single-game and single-season NHL records chronologically by when they were set (earliest first).
events: Joe Malone scores 10 goals in a single game (1920)
Mud Bruneteau ends the longest game in NHL history after 176 minutes (1936)
Bill Mosienko scores the fastest hat trick in NHL history, in 21 seconds (1952)
Wayne Gretzky scores 50 goals in 39 games (1981-82)
explanation: Order: Malone 1920, Bruneteau 1936, Mosienko 1952, Gretzky 1981-82.

prompt: Which team recorded the fastest two goals scored by one team, netting twice in just 4 seconds during a December 1987 game against St. Louis?
explanation: The Boston Bruins scored twice in 4 seconds in a December 1987 game, an NHL record for a team.

prompt: Which player scored the most career regular-season overtime goals in NHL history?
explanation: Ovechkin has scored more regular-season overtime goals than any other player, especially since the 3-on-3 format began in 2015.

prompt: Which format did the NHL adopt for regular-season overtime starting in the 2015-16 season, replacing 4-on-4?
explanation: The 3-on-3 format was introduced to create more scoring chances and reduce the number of games decided by shootout.

prompt: Which format did NHL regular-season overtime use before switching to 3-on-3 in 2015?
explanation: 4-on-4 overtime was used from 1999 (when the shootout format hadn't been introduced yet) through 2015.

prompt: In what year did the NHL introduce the shootout to decide regular-season games tied after overtime?
explanation: The shootout was introduced for the 2005-06 season after the lockout that canceled the previous year.

prompt: Which lockout wiped out the entire 2004-05 NHL season?
options: No lockout has ever canceled a full season
explanation: The 2004-05 lockout was the first time a major North American sports league canceled an entire season due to a labor dispute.

prompt: Which 2012-13 NHL season was shortened to 48 games due to a lockout?
explanation: A lockout delayed the start of the 2012-13 season, shortening it to 48 games per team.

prompt: Which player was suspended for the entire following playoffs after a headshot on Marian Hossa in the 2011 postseason?
explanation: Raffi Torres received one of the longest suspensions in NHL history for his hit on Hossa.

prompt: Which player received a 25-game suspension (later reduced) for a devastating hit on Steve Moore in 2004, effectively ending Moore's career?
explanation: Bertuzzi's attack on Moore led to a long legal battle and remains one of the most infamous incidents in NHL history.

prompt: Which enforcer holds the NHL record for most career penalty minutes, with 3,971?
explanation: Dave Williams's penalty minute total remains the highest in NHL history.

prompt: Which team drafted Wayne Gretzky, only for him to never actually play for them due to the WHA's dissolution and a subsequent trade?
options: Indianapolis Racers (WHA), before being sold to Edmonton
explanation: Gretzky briefly played for the Indianapolis Racers before being sold to the Edmonton Oilers in the WHA's final season.

prompt: Which league, a rival to the NHL in the 1970s, merged four of its teams into the NHL in 1979?
options: American Hockey League (a minor league, not a rival)
explanation: The WHA operated from 1972 to 1979 before folding, with four teams joining the NHL.

prompt: Which legendary player came out of retirement to play for the WHA's Houston Aeros and Winnipeg Jets alongside his son in the 1970s?
explanation: Gordie Howe played into his 50s in the WHA, including alongside his son Mark on the Aeros.

prompt: Which star player left the NHL's Chicago Black Hawks for the rival WHA's Winnipeg Jets in 1972, signing a then-record contract?
explanation: Bobby Hull's jump to the WHA for a $1 million-plus contract was a landmark moment in hockey labor history.

prompt: Which NHL playoff MVP trophy has, on rare occasions, been awarded to a player from the team that lost the Stanley Cup Final?
explanation: The Conn Smythe Trophy has occasionally gone to a player on the losing team, such as Jean-Sebastien Giguere in 2003.

prompt: Which goaltender won the Conn Smythe Trophy as playoff MVP in 2003 despite his team, the Anaheim Ducks, losing the Stanley Cup Final?
explanation: Giguere's stellar play was recognized with the Conn Smythe even though Anaheim lost to New Jersey in the Final.

prompt: By what score did the Boston Bruins win the decisive Game 7 of the 2011 Stanley Cup Final against Vancouver?
explanation: Boston shut out Vancouver 4-0 in Game 7 to win the franchise's first title since 1972.

prompt: Which 2019 Stanley Cup Final Game 7 between St. Louis and Boston was decided by what final score?
explanation: The Blues clinched their first championship with a 4-1 win in Boston to close out the series.

prompt: Which arena is nicknamed 'The Gardens' and was the historic home of the Toronto Maple Leafs until 1999?
options: Air Canada Centre (its immediate successor's original name)
explanation: Maple Leaf Gardens hosted the Leafs from 1931 until they moved to the Air Canada Centre in 1999.

prompt: Which historic Montreal arena, home to the Canadiens' dynasty years, was replaced by the Bell Centre in 1996?
explanation: The Montreal Forum hosted the Canadiens for most of their championship history before the move to the Bell Centre.

prompt: Which historic Boston arena, known for its cramped seating and unique atmosphere, was replaced by the current TD Garden in 1995?
explanation: The old Boston Garden was famous for its distinctive parquet-adjacent atmosphere before being replaced.

prompt: Which award is presented to the player selected as the best in the NHL All-Star Game each year?
explanation: The All-Star Game MVP is a separate honor from the regular season and playoff awards.

prompt: Which award, voted on by fellow NHL players, recognizes the league's most outstanding player each season?
options: Hart Trophy (voted by media/broadcasters, not players)
Lester Pearson Award (former name of the same award)
explanation: The Ted Lindsay Award (formerly the Lester B. Pearson Award) is unique in being voted on by the NHL Players' Association.

prompt: What was the Ted Lindsay Award previously known as, before being renamed in 2010?
explanation: The award was renamed from the Lester B. Pearson Award to honor former player Ted Lindsay.

prompt: Which country won the inaugural World Cup of Hockey in 1996, a best-on-best tournament that replaced the old Canada Cup?
explanation: The US beat Canada in the final to win the first World Cup of Hockey in 1996.

prompt: Which tournament, first held in 1976, was the precursor to the World Cup of Hockey and featured best-on-best international competition?
explanation: The Canada Cup ran from 1976 to 1991 before being rebranded as the World Cup of Hockey.

prompt: Which historic 1972 series between Canada and the Soviet Union is considered one of the most important events in hockey history?
explanation: The 1972 Summit Series, won dramatically by Canada, changed how the hockey world viewed Soviet players.

prompt: Which Canadian player scored the legendary series-winning goal against the Soviet Union in the final game of the 1972 Summit Series?
explanation: Paul Henderson's goal with 34 seconds remaining is one of the most iconic moments in Canadian sports history.

prompt: Which alternative all-star exhibition series between NHL players and the Soviet national team was held in 1979, considered a de facto changing of the guard?
explanation: The 1979 Challenge Cup saw the Soviets defeat an NHL All-Star team, signaling their rising dominance.

prompt: Which major goaltending trophy is unique in being decided by a vote of NHL general managers, rather than media or players?
explanation: The Vezina Trophy is the only major goaltending award voted on by general managers.

prompt: Which trophy is awarded to the goaltender(s) on the team that allowed the fewest goals during the regular season?
explanation: The Jennings Trophy is a statistical, team-based award, distinct from the more subjective Vezina.

prompt: Which player, drafted first overall in 1984 by Pittsburgh, is widely regarded as one of the two or three greatest players ever, alongside Gretzky?
explanation: Lemieux's combination of size, skill, and scoring touch made him a generational talent for Pittsburgh.

prompt: Which player, drafted first overall in 1991 amid controversy over his refusal to play for the Quebec Nordiques, was traded to Philadelphia before ever playing for Quebec?
explanation: Lindros's refusal to play for Quebec led to a blockbuster trade to the Philadelphia Flyers.

prompt: Which player, drafted first overall in 1993, became a symbol of draft-bust risk after failing to live up to expectations with Ottawa?
explanation: Daigle's underwhelming career after being picked first overall is often cited as a cautionary tale in draft analysis.

prompt: Which player, drafted first overall in 1999, is often cited alongside Daigle as one of the biggest draft busts in NHL history?
explanation: Patrik Stefan's underwhelming NHL career, including a famous empty-net blunder, made him a symbol of draft risk.

prompt: Which player, selected first overall by the New York Islanders in 2000, was a goaltender — a rare choice for the top pick?
explanation: DiPietro remains one of the very few goaltenders ever selected first overall in the NHL Draft.

prompt: Which goaltender was Pittsburgh's first-overall draft pick in 2003, three years before the franchise also landed Sidney Crosby?
explanation: Fleury became a franchise cornerstone, winning three Stanley Cups with Pittsburgh.

statement: Paul Henderson scored the legendary series-winning goal for Canada in the final game of the 1972 Summit Series.
explanation: Henderson's goal with 34 seconds left remains iconic in Canadian sports history.

statement: The NHL introduced the shootout for regular-season games starting in the 2005-06 season.
explanation: This followed the lockout that canceled the entire 2004-05 season.

statement: Jean-Sebastien Giguere won the Conn Smythe Trophy in 2003 despite his team losing the Stanley Cup Final.
explanation: Giguere's exceptional play for Anaheim was recognized even though the Ducks lost to New Jersey.

statement: Bobby Hull's jump from the NHL to the rival WHA in 1972 involved a then-record contract.
explanation: Hull's signing with the Winnipeg Jets for over $1 million was a landmark moment in hockey history.

statement: Gordie Howe played in the WHA alongside his son during the 1970s after initially retiring from the NHL.
explanation: Howe's comeback with the Houston Aeros included playing alongside his son Mark.

statement: The Ted Lindsay Award is voted on by NHL players themselves, unlike the media-voted Hart Trophy.
explanation: This distinction makes the Ted Lindsay Award unique among major individual NHL honors.

statement: Rick DiPietro, selected first overall by the Islanders in 2000, was a goaltender.
explanation: Goaltenders are rarely picked first overall, making DiPietro's selection notable.

statement: The Jennings Trophy is awarded based on a vote by NHL general managers.
explanation: The Jennings Trophy is a statistical award for the fewest goals allowed; the Vezina Trophy is the one voted on by general managers.

statement: The 2004-05 NHL lockout resulted in the entire season being canceled.
explanation: This marked the first time a major North American sports league lost an entire season to a labor dispute.

statement: Eric Lindros was traded to Philadelphia after refusing to play for the team that drafted him, Quebec.
explanation: Lindros's holdout led to one of the most significant trades in NHL history.

Drafted first overall in 1984 by Pittsburgh
Won two Stanley Cups and multiple scoring titles
Widely considered one of the two or three greatest players ever
explanation: Lemieux's combination of size and skill made him a generational talent despite a career shortened by injuries and illness.

clues: Canadian forward
Scored the series-winning goal in the 1972 Summit Series
Played for Team Canada against the Soviet Union
His goal came with 34 seconds remaining in the deciding game
explanation: Henderson's goal remains one of the most celebrated moments in Canadian hockey history.

clues: American-born goaltender
Won the Conn Smythe Trophy in 2003 despite his team losing the Final
Played for the Anaheim Ducks
Later played for Toronto and Colorado
explanation: Giguere's playoff performance in 2003 remains one of the greatest individual goaltending runs in NHL history despite the team's loss.

clues: Canadian forward
Refused to play for the team that drafted him first overall in 1991
Traded to Philadelphia before playing a game for that team
Nicknamed 'The Big E'
explanation: Lindros's holdout and subsequent trade was one of the most dramatic stories in NHL draft history.

prompt: Order these historic international hockey events chronologically (earliest first).
events: The Summit Series between Canada and the Soviet Union (1972)
The Challenge Cup between the Soviets and NHL All-Stars (1979)
The first Canada Cup precursor tournament format ends and the World Cup of Hockey begins (1996)
Canada wins Olympic gold on home ice in Vancouver (2010)
explanation: Order: Summit Series 1972, Challenge Cup 1979, World Cup of Hockey begins 1996, Vancouver gold 2010.
